finding.title: Start-timeout cleanup block terminates/marks without re-checking status or identity: kills a healthy session at the 5s boundary, orphans it, or signals a recycled PID
finding.detail:
wait_for_running_metadata's timeout branch (src/session.rs:267-278), added by the m11/m12/m24 fix, runs after the 50-iteration (~5s) poll loop: it reloads metadata and UNCONDITIONALLY calls terminate_pid(supervisor_pid) (line 271), terminate_pid(child_pid) (line 274), then mark_stopped(name, Some("start timed out"), None) (line 277), without (a) re-checking that status is still Starting and without (b) any pid_is_ours guard — unlike stop_session_silent (session.rs:618,625), which the m5/c2 fix specifically gated. Three concrete failures stem from this one block:

1. KILL-HEALTHY-AT-BOUNDARY (both modes). A slow-starting command (cold-start claude on a loaded host / slow openpty/spawn/atomic-write) has supervise_pty publish status=Running with a live, genuinely-ours child_pid (session.rs:364-368) in the window after the loop's final read but before the cleanup reload. The reload sees Running with a live child; terminate_pid SIGTERM/SIGKILLs it (and its process group via -pid), mark_stopped flips the just-started healthy session to Stopped, and the caller bails 'did not report running within 5 seconds'. In daemon mode the owner thread (daemon.rs:236-246) runs supervise_pty + mark_stopped WITHOUT the start lock while the handler holds it, so the two mark_stopped writes also race into a lost update on exit_status/exit_code. Baseline (d230009) only bail!ed here and killed nothing, so a slow-but-alive start survived and stayed controllable — the fix turned 'slow but alive' into 'actively destroyed'.

2. ORPHAN-AFTER-MARK-STOPPED (daemon mode). supervisor_pid is None in daemon mode, so the block can only terminate child_pid. If the cleanup's load_metadata observes the pre-publish snapshot (Starting, child_pid=None) microseconds before the supervisor thread publishes Running, it terminates nothing, then mark_stopped writes Stopped. The supervisor thread has already passed its single Stopped-check (session.rs:360), so the child now runs unmanaged while metadata says Stopped: status reports Stopped but ps shows the child + its capture/input threads alive, stop is a no-op (stop_session_silent early-returns on Stopped, session.rs:610), read keeps growing, and the orphan only self-heals when the child exits on its own.

3. RECYCLED-PID KILL (both modes). When the start genuinely timed out (still Starting, supervisor/child died), the recorded supervisor_pid/child_pid may have been recycled by the kernel to an unrelated process before the loop ends. terminate_pid -> signal_pid_and_group signals both pid and -pid (session.rs:674) with SIGTERM then SIGKILL, killing a stranger's process and its entire process group. This is exactly the PID-reuse kill that m5/c2/process_start_time tokens were introduced to prevent, but this path ignores the recorded start-time tokens entirely.
finding.anchor: wait_for_running_metadata timeout branch, src/session.rs:267-278 (terminate_pid at 271/274, mark_stopped at 277) vs supervise_pty publish at src/session.rs:360-368 and the daemon owner thread at src/daemon.rs:236-246
finding.fix: Make the timeout converge cooperatively and safely: reload metadata and return Ok(metadata) if status already raced to Running (and treat Stopped via the existing exit_code==0 / failure logic); only when status is still Starting should it terminate, and each terminate_pid call must be gated behind pid_is_ours(pid, matching_start_time) so a recycled PID is never signaled. Keep the single owner thread as the sole writer of terminal state (CAS on status under the per-session start lock) rather than killing+marking from the waiter, eliminating the double-mark_stopped race.

verdict.reasoning:
The timeout branch of wait_for_running_metadata (src/session.rs:267-278) is genuinely unguarded compared to stop_session_silent: it (a) never re-checks that status is still Starting before acting, and (b) calls terminate_pid(supervisor_pid) (271) and terminate_pid(child_pid) (274) WITHOUT a pid_is_ours guard, then mark_stopped (277) unconditionally. stop_session_silent gates both terminate_pid calls behind pid_is_ours (session.rs:618,625) and early-returns on Stopped (610). This asymmetry is real and is the root cause the claim names.

Concurrency is unsynchronized on the scenario path. The per-session start lock (acquire_start_lock) is held only by the CLI caller (direct mode) or the daemon request handler (daemon.rs:223); the supervisor — a separate process in direct mode (run_supervisor) and a detached thread in daemon mode (daemon.rs:236) — never takes that lock. So supervise_pty's publish of Running (session.rs:364-368, via write_atomic rename) races wait_for_running_metadata with no coordination. mark_stopped (689-696) only flips status; it does not signal, and supervise_pty checks Stopped exactly once at line 360 before publishing.

Confirmed failure (sub-claim 1, kill-healthy): at the 5s boundary, if supervise_pty's save_metadata(Running) rename lands in the gap between the loop's final poll read (line 247, iteration 50, which saw Starting) and the reload at line 269, the reload observes Running with a live, genuinely-ours child; terminate_pid SIGTERM/SIGKILLs it and its group (-pid via signal_pid_and_group, 674), and mark_stopped flips the just-started healthy session to Stopped. Confirmed failure (sub-claim 2, orphan, daemon mode): if Running publishes in the narrower gap between the reload (269, sees child_pid=None so terminate is skipped) and mark_stopped's own internal load_metadata (690), mark_stopped re-reads Running and forces Stopped WITHOUT killing — leaving the child plus its capture_output/forward_input threads alive while metadata says Stopped; stop_session_silent then no-ops (610), so the child is unmanaged and unkillable via stop until it self-exits. Both are real with observable bad consequences.

Regression confirmed against baseline d230009 (src/main.rs:665-683): the timeout branch was a bare bail! that killed/marked nothing, so a slow-but-alive start survived and stayed controllable. The m11/m12/m24 fix added the terminate+mark block. However, the DETERMINISTIC >5s-startup kill that the claim leans on for 'high' is the intended m24 behavior (commit 13cdda5: 'a start that times out still in Starting now terminates the in-flight supervisor/child and marks Stopped'), which is a defensible design choice (no ghost sessions), so it is not itself the finding. What remains as the genuine defect is the TOCTOU at the 5s boundary: the branch acts on a stale snapshot and lacks the status re-check + pid_is_ours guard, so a session crossing into Running exactly as the waiter gives up is killed or orphaned. Sub-claim 3 (recycled-PID kill) is technically valid — the branch ignores the start-time tokens that m5/c2 added and signal_pid_and_group signals -pid too — but full PID wraparound within the ~5s window is implausible on a normal host, so it is low-probability hardening on its own.

Severity medium, not high: the trigger is a sub-millisecond-to-low-millisecond race window at the 5s boundary (loop-exit/reload gap with no sleep), correlated with heavy load (which both lengthens the gap and causes near-5s starts), and the consequence is a healthy session killed or left as stale Stopped-but-alive state — squarely the rubric's 'medium = race / crash leaving stale state' bucket. The double-mark_stopped lost-update (daemon waiter mark_stopped at 277 vs daemon thread mark_stopped at daemon.rs:243) is real but only perturbs exit_status/exit_code text (both write Stopped), so it does not raise severity.

finding.title: Daemon shutdown can orphan a session whose child was just spawned but not yet recorded (process::exit races the supervisor thread)
finding.detail: shutdown_sessions_for_daemon now includes Starting sessions (daemon.rs:267-271, the m24 'don't orphan mid-startup' fix) and calls stop_session_silent on each, then handle_daemon_stream does std::process::exit(0) (daemon.rs:146). But in daemon mode the supervisor is a thread, so supervisor_pid is always None, and there is a window in supervise_pty between spawning the child (session.rs:336) and recording child_pid into metadata (session.rs:365-368). If shutdown runs in that window, stop_session_silent finds child_pid==None and supervisor_pid==None, so pid_is_ours is false for both (session.rs:618,625): it signals nothing and just marks the session Stopped. The child is reaped only if the supervisor thread reaches the m22 'was stopped during startup' bail (session.rs:360) and its reap (session.rs:408) BEFORE process::exit(0) tears the daemon (and that thread) down. process::exit races the supervisor thread and can win, leaving the just-spawned child as an orphan. PTY-master close on daemon exit delivers SIGHUP, which kills most programs, but a child that ignores SIGHUP (or re-execs) survives unreaped and unmanaged — exactly the orphan m24 set out to prevent. Observable: after `shutdown`, a stray process keeps running with no owning daemon and a session dir marked Stopped.
finding.anchor: shutdown_sessions_for_daemon (src/daemon.rs:251-292) + process::exit (src/daemon.rs:146) vs the unrecorded-child window in supervise_pty (src/session.rs:336-368)
finding.fix: Record child_pid into metadata immediately after spawn (before opening the FIFO / status check) so stop_session_silent can always signal it, and/or have the daemon join outstanding supervisor threads (or signal each session's PTY child process group) before process::exit so a mid-spawn child is reliably terminated.

verdict.reasoning:
The race is real and reachable in daemon mode. Walking the failure scenario against current code:

1. Window exists. In supervise_pty (src/session.rs) the child is spawned at line 336 (pair.slave.spawn_command), but child_pid is only recorded and status flipped to Running together at lines 364-368. In between there is a FIFO open (348-354) and a load_metadata (356). So for the entire time a session's status is Starting, its persisted child_pid is None — there is NO persisted state of status==Starting with child_pid==Some.

2. Daemon mode has no supervisor PID. supervisor_pid is set only in run_supervisor (session.rs:285-287), which is the direct-mode separate-process path. start_session_in_daemon (daemon.rs:236-246) runs supervise_pty on a thread and never sets supervisor_pid, so in daemon mode it is always None (confirmed by grep: only writers are run_supervisor and a test).

3. Shutdown can't signal the mid-spawn child. shutdown_sessions_for_daemon now includes Starting sessions (daemon.rs:267-271, the m24 change) and calls stop_session_silent. For a mid-spawn daemon session, stop_session_silent (session.rs:604-639) sees status!=Stopped, then pid_is_ours(child_pid=None,...) is false (session.rs:733 early-return on None) and pid_is_ours(supervisor_pid=None,...) is false, so it signals nothing and only calls mark_stopped (session.rs:636). m24's inclusion of Starting sessions therefore performs zero termination for a daemon-mode mid-spawn child; it merely flips metadata to Stopped.

4. process::exit races and can win. handle_daemon_stream then calls std::process::exit(0) (daemon.rs:146). I empirically verified (tiny rustc probe under /tmp) that std::process::exit terminates the process WITHOUT joining spawned threads — the supervisor thread's "was stopped during startup" bail (session.rs:360-362) and its reap (child.kill()/child.wait() at 407-409) are abandoned if exit wins.

5. Only safety net is PTY SIGHUP, which is partial. portable-pty calls setsid()+TIOCSCTTY in the child pre_exec (portable-pty-0.8.1/src/unix.rs:220,234), so the child is a session leader on the slave; when the daemon exits and the kernel closes the master fd, the child's process group gets SIGHUP. That kills SIGHUP-default programs but NOT children that catch/ignore SIGHUP (Claude Code, dev servers, many TUIs) or re-exec — which survive unreaped/unmanaged. Observable: after `shutdown`, a stray process keeps running with the session dir marked Stopped — the orphan m24 aimed to prevent.

Severity = medium (not raised to a regression floor): this is NOT a regression. At baseline (src/main.rs@d230009) shutdown_sessions_for_daemon included only Running sessions (line 1046), so a mid-spawn Starting session was never touched, there was no m22 bail, and process::exit raced identically — the same child was orphaned the same way. m24 did not make it worse; it closed the analogous direct-mode case (where supervisor_pid IS set and gets signaled) but left the daemon-mode mid-spawn sub-window open. The consequence is a narrow triple-coincidence race (shutdown landing in a sub-ms/few-ms per-start window AND process::exit winning the thread race AND the child catching SIGHUP) with a recoverable, non-catastrophic outcome (one stray process, no data loss, no cross-user security boundary), which fits medium: failure under plausible-but-less-common conditions leaving a stale process.
verdict.fix: Record child_pid (and child_start_time) into metadata immediately after spawn_command (src/session.rs:336), BEFORE the FIFO open and the status/Stopped check, instead of only at lines 365-366. Keep status as Starting at that point; flip to Running later as today. With child_pid persisted early, a concurrent shutdown's stop_session_silent will find pid_is_ours(child_pid, child_start_time) true and call terminate_pid(child_pid), which signals the child's own process group (-child_pid; valid because portable-pty setsid's the child) with SIGTERM/SIGKILL on the shutdown thread BEFORE it reaches std::process::exit(0). That guarantees the just-spawned child is terminated regardless of whether the supervisor thread wins or loses the exit race. (Heavier alternatives: have the daemon track and join outstanding supervisor threads, or iterate sessions and signal each PTY child's process group, before process::exit — but the early child_pid record is the minimal correct fix.)

finding.title: Old supervisor thread's terminal mark_stopped clobbers a freshly-restarted daemon session, bricking its control plane
finding.detail: In daemon mode the supervisor runs as a thread; on child exit it calls mark_stopped(name,...) (daemon.rs:243), an unconditional load-modify-write of metadata (mark_stopped, session.rs:689-695) with no identity/generation guard. Scenario: session 'foo' is Running with old child C1 owned by thread T1. C1 exits on its own, so T1 returns from child.wait() and is scheduled to call mark_stopped, but the scheduler hasn't run it yet. A manager agent restarts the session the moment it sees the child died: `start foo`. The start lock is free (T1 holds no lock), session_is_active() returns false because pid_is_ours(C1) sees C1 dead (m5), so the restart proceeds: initialize_session_files rotates logs and writes fresh Starting metadata, and a new thread T2 spawns child C2, sets status=Running, child_pid=C2 (supervise_pty, session.rs:364-368). NOW T1 finally runs mark_stopped: it loads the current (T2/Running/C2) metadata, overwrites status=Stopped with C1's exit_status/exit_code, and saves. The live restarted session is now recorded Stopped. Because write_session_bytes requires status==Running (session.rs:455), every subsequent send/keys fails 'session is not running' even though C2 and its forwarder are alive; C2 is unreachable from the control plane (and leaks) until another restart. wait_for_running_metadata for the T2 start can also observe the clobbered Stopped state and bail 'stopped while starting' or, if C1 exited 0, falsely print 'ran to completion (exit code 0)' for a live restart. Baseline had the same threading, but the fixes (m5 reliably permitting restart of a dead-child Running session + the strict Running gate on send) make this lost update concretely brick an otherwise-healthy session.
finding.anchor: mark_stopped, src/session.rs:689-695 (clobber) interacting with start_session_in_daemon's spawned thread, src/daemon.rs:243, and supervise_pty publish at src/session.rs:364-368
finding.fix: Have mark_stopped (or the daemon thread's terminal call) only transition to Stopped when the metadata still identifies the same run — compare child_pid/child_start_time (or a per-start generation/epoch stamped into metadata) and skip the write if they no longer match the run that thread owned.

verdict.reasoning:
The lost-update race is real and reachable in the CURRENT daemon code. Mechanism, walked against real lines:

1. In daemon mode a session is owned by a DETACHED thread spawned at daemon.rs:236; on child exit it calls mark_stopped at daemon.rs:243. There is NO thread registry, no join of old threads, no Mutex/RwLock around metadata, no SIGCHLD reaper, and no generation/epoch field (grep over src/ confirms: the only join() is the unrelated input forwarder at session.rs:390).

2. mark_stopped (session.rs:689-695) is an unconditional load->set Stopped->write_atomic with NO identity guard — it does not compare child_pid/child_start_time against the run the thread owned.

3. Restart of a dead-child Running session is permitted: session_is_active (session.rs:748-752) is `status==Running && (pid_is_ours(supervisor) || pid_is_ours(child))`. Critically, in daemon mode supervisor_pid is never set — supervise_pty is called directly (daemon.rs:238) and only writes child_pid/child_start_time (session.rs:365-366); supervisor_pid stays None (initialize_session_files sets it None at session.rs:216; the only Some(pid) write is run_supervisor session.rs:286, the DIRECT-mode path). So pid_is_ours(None)=false (session.rs:733-735) and, with dead C1, pid_is_ours(C1)=false -> session_is_active=false -> restart proceeds. The per-session start lock (daemon.rs:223) does not help: T1 holds no lock.

Failure interleaving: C1 self-exits; T1 returns from child.wait() (session.rs:383), runs the up-to-2s output drain (session.rs:397-400), supervise_pty returns Ok, but T1 has not yet reached mark_stopped. Manager observes child_alive=false (status_text, session.rs:535) and issues `start foo`. start_session_in_daemon initializes fresh Starting metadata, T2 spawns C2 and publishes status=Running/child_pid=C2 (the Stopped-guard at session.rs:360 passes because metadata is Starting, not Stopped). wait_for_running_metadata returns success. THEN T1 finally runs mark_stopped: loads the live T2/Running/C2 metadata and overwrites status=Stopped (last-writer-wins via write_atomic rename). The healthy restarted session is now recorded Stopped, so every send/keys hits the strict gate write_session_bytes -> `status != Running -> bail "session is not running"` (session.rs:454-456). C2 and its forwarder are alive but unreachable; a further `start` to recover does NOT kill C2 (initialize_session_files only unlinks/recreates the FIFO, never signals the prior child), so C2 leaks as an orphaned PTY child. wait_for_running_metadata for the T2 start can also observe the clobbered Stopped and bail 'stopped while starting', or falsely report 'ran to completion (exit code 0)' if C1 exited 0 (session.rs:251-263).

One correction to the claim's framing: this is NOT a regression and the named amplifiers are not actually new. Baseline (d230009:src/main.rs) guarded restart on `status==Running && child_pid process_alive` (false for dead C1) and had the identical strict send gate `status != Running -> bail` (line 873-874), with the same detached-thread mark_stopped model. So the race, its reachability, and the bricking consequence all pre-existed identically; m5's start-time tokens and the send gate did not change reachability or impact here. It is a genuine pre-existing concurrency defect surfaced by the re-review, not something the fixes introduced or worsened. Severity therefore rests on the bug's own character: a specific-but-plausible interleaving (auto-restart on observed child death is the documented manager pattern) within a narrow window that leaves stale on-disk state (session wrongly Stopped) plus an orphaned/leaked child — exactly the "races / crashes leaving stale state" profile of medium. Claimed medium stands; only the regression/fix-interaction attribution is inaccurate.
verdict.fix: Make the terminal Stopped transition identity-aware so a stale thread cannot clobber a newer run. Concretely, change mark_stopped (session.rs:689) to take the identity of the run the caller owned and skip the write if metadata no longer matches. Simplest correct version using existing fields: pass the child_pid/child_start_time that supervise_pty published (return them, or have the daemon thread read them) and in mark_stopped do `let m = load_metadata(name)?; if m.child_pid != owned_child_pid || m.child_start_time != owned_child_start_time { return Ok(()); }` before setting Stopped. Even more robust: add a monotonically increasing `generation: u64` to SessionMetadata stamped in initialize_session_files and re-read by supervise_pty before publishing Running; mark_stopped then writes Stopped only if the loaded generation equals the one that thread owned. Note both stop_session_silent (session.rs:636) and wait_for_running_metadata's timeout (session.rs:277) also call mark_stopped, so guard the load-modify-write inside mark_stopped (or a shared helper) rather than only at the daemon.rs:243 call site; those callers hold the start identity or, for user-stop, already verified pid_is_ours, so a generation/identity match keeps them working. As a hardening complement, when a restart proceeds over a previous Running run whose child is dead, the new initialize_session_files path could also best-effort terminate any still-live prior child_pid to avoid leaking a child created by a racing thread.

finding.title: PID-reuse defense (m5) is bypassed for the child when its start-time token fails to record at publish time
finding.detail: supervise_pty records child identity as child_pid = child.process_id() and child_start_time = child.process_id().and_then(process_start_time) (session.rs:365-366). If process_start_time returns None at that instant (the child already exited and proc_pidinfo/proc-stat declines to report a token, or a transient proc_pidinfo failure on macOS), metadata ends up with child_pid=Some(pid) but child_start_time=None. Later, pid_is_ours(Some(pid), None) hits the legacy fallback branch (session.rs:739-742) and returns true on bare liveness alone. stop_session_silent and shutdown gate the kill on pid_is_ours (session.rs:618) and, because signal_pid_and_group also signals the negative pid (session.rs:674), send SIGTERM/SIGKILL to that pid AND its process group. If the OS recycled that exact PID to an unrelated process after the child died, stop/shutdown/daemon-exit kills an innocent process group. This is precisely the PID-reuse hole m5 set out to close, but the token-vs-no-token fallback was meant for legacy on-disk metadata, not for freshly written metadata where the token query merely failed. The window is narrow (token must fail to record for a live-then-dead child, the PID must be recycled, and the user must run stop), but the consequence is killing the wrong process group.
finding.anchor: supervise_pty child identity write, src/session.rs:365-366; pid_is_ours fallback, src/session.rs:739-742
finding.fix: If child_pid is Some but process_start_time yields None at publish time, treat the identity as untrusted (e.g. refuse to signal that PID, or fail the start) instead of silently falling back to a bare-liveness kill for current (non-legacy) sessions.

verdict.reasoning:
Every link in the scenario verifies against current code plus a confirmed macOS kernel behavior.

1. child.process_id() always returns Some(pid) even after the child has exited (pre-reap). portable-pty 0.8.1's spawn_command returns std::process::Child (unix.rs:194), and impl Child for std::process::Child maps process_id() to Some(self.id()) (lib.rs:272-274). std::process::Child::id() never reaps and always returns the stored PID.

2. process_start_time(pid) returns None for a just-exited (zombie) child on macOS. I proved this with a /tmp probe: fork a child that _exit(0)s, do NOT wait, then call proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDTBSDINFO,...) -> returns 0, not size=136. procinfo.rs:33-37 returns None unless written==size. (A live process returns 136 -> Some, confirmed by a second probe.) So if the child dies between spawn (session.rs:339) and the token read (session.rs:366) -- a fast-exit / failed-exec at startup -- save_metadata at session.rs:368 persists status=Running, child_pid=Some(pid), child_start_time=None. With #[serde(default)] on child_start_time (session.rs:54-55), this round-trips as null -> None.

3. pid_is_ours(Some(pid), None) returns true on bare liveness: session.rs:739-742, the None => true arm after process_alive() (line 736). For a recycled PID, process_alive (kill(pid,0)) is true.

4. A concurrent stop/shutdown acts during the window. stop_session_silent only early-returns when status==Stopped (session.rs:610); during the window status is still Running. Each daemon connection runs in its own thread (daemon.rs:64); nothing serializes request-handling against the supervisor thread -- the per-session acquire_start_lock is a local in start_session_in_daemon (daemon.rs:223) released when that function returns (line 248), and stop_session_silent never takes it. The materially dangerous sub-window is after child.wait() reaps the zombie (session.rs:383) through the up-to-2s output drain (session.rs:397-400) until mark_stopped flips to Stopped (daemon.rs:243): here the PID is reaped and recyclable while metadata still says Running + null token.

5. Consequence: pid_is_ours true -> terminate_pid(pid) -> signal_pid_and_group sends SIGTERM then SIGKILL to BOTH Pid::from_raw(-pid) (the process group) and Pid::from_raw(pid) (session.rs:674). If the OS recycled that exact PID to an unrelated process, its whole process group is signaled. This is the PID-reuse hole m5 set out to close.

Not a regression: baseline d230009 had no start-time token at all and stop_session_silent killed child_pid unconditionally on any recorded PID with no liveness/Stopped gate (git show d230009:src/main.rs lines 1078-1100), i.e. strictly worse. So this is correctly an incomplete fix of m5: the token-vs-no-token fallback was intended for legacy on-disk metadata, but it also silently covers freshly written metadata where the token query merely failed for a live-then-zombie child.

Refinement vs. the claim: this affects BOTH direct and daemon mode (both call supervise_pty -> line 366; stop_session_silent independently gates and kills child_pid at session.rs:618-623 regardless of the supervisor token). Severity is medium, not high: the exploit needs a conjunction -- a child that exits within ~1ms of spawn (fast-exit / failed exec), the token thus recording as null, a concurrent stop/shutdown landing in the short reaped-and-recyclable window (typically tens of ms; up to 2s only when a backgrounded grandchild holds the PTY), AND the kernel recycling that exact PID in that window. Real PID-reuse kill of an innocent same-user process group, but behind a tight race -- matching the rubric's medium band (failure under plausible-but-less-common race conditions).

finding.title: AnsiCleaner does not treat ESC as cancel/restart, leaking control fragments into the clean log
finding.detail: clean::AnsiCleaner::step never honors the ECMA-48/vte rule that an ESC byte (0x1b) aborts any in-progress sequence and re-enters the Escape state. In State::Escape, escape() routes a second ESC through the `_ => State::Ground` arm (clean.rs:107), consuming it as if it were a short escape's final byte. In State::Csi (clean.rs:57) and State::EscapeIntermediate (clean.rs:52) an ESC is simply not in the tested terminator range, so it is swallowed while the state persists. Concrete failure: a program emits a CSI interrupted by a fresh CSI, e.g. "\x1b[01;3\x1b[0m" (partial SGR, then a reset). Trace: ESC->Escape, '['->Csi, '0','1',';','3' stay Csi, the second ESC is swallowed (stays Csi), then '[' (0x5b, which IS in the 0x40..=0x7e final-byte range) falsely terminates the first CSI -> Ground, and the trailing '0','m' are emitted as literal text. The human-readable clean.log (and read/--tail over it) then shows a stray '0m' fragment; "\x1b\x1b[0m" similarly leaks '[0m'. The vt100-rendered screen snapshot handles this correctly (vte restarts on ESC), so only the clean log diverges; raw.log is unaffected and no real text is lost or UTF-8 corrupted. Baseline used strip_ansi_escapes::strip (vte-based), which DOES restart on ESC and would have stripped these fragments — so for ESC-interrupted sequences this is a fidelity regression introduced by the c1/m16 rewrite (which in exchange correctly fixed the baseline's worse per-chunk split-sequence leak).
finding.anchor: AnsiCleaner::step + AnsiCleaner::escape, src/clean.rs:48-77 (escape arm at clean.rs:100-109, Csi at clean.rs:57-63, EscapeIntermediate at clean.rs:52-56)
finding.fix: At the top of step(), special-case byte == 0x1b to set self.state = State::Escape and return for every state except the ST-completion states (Osc->OscEsc and StringSeq->StringEsc, which already handle ESC), so an ESC anywhere cancels the current sequence and restarts parsing exactly as vte does.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real (but cosmetic, narrow-trigger) regression in src/clean.rs.

Mechanism, verified line-by-line against current code:
- State::Csi (clean.rs:57-63) ends only on a final byte 0x40..=0x7e. ESC (0x1b) is below that range, so a mid-CSI ESC is swallowed and the Csi state persists.
- State::EscapeIntermediate (clean.rs:52-56) ends only on 0x30..=0x7e; ESC is swallowed there too.
- State::Escape (escape(), clean.rs:100-109): a second ESC is not '[', ']', P/X/^/_, nor 0x20..=0x2f, so it hits `_ => State::Ground`, i.e. it is consumed as if it were a short escape's final byte rather than restarting.
None of these treat ESC as the ECMA-48/vte cancel+restart it should be.

I empirically confirmed the failure AND the regression by building two throwaway probes in /tmp (now deleted; project tree never touched):
1) The current AnsiCleaner logic, copied verbatim from clean.rs, produces:
   - input "\x1b[01;3\x1b[0m"  -> clean log "0m"   (stray fragment leaks)
   - input "\x1b\x1b[0m"        -> clean log "[0m"  (stray fragment leaks)
   Trace for case 1: ESC->Escape, '['->Csi, '0''1'';''3' stay Csi, second ESC swallowed (stays Csi), then '[' (0x5b, which IS in 0x40..=0x7e) falsely terminates the first CSI -> Ground, and trailing '0''m' are emitted as literal text. Exactly the claim's trace.
2) The actual baseline dependency strip_ansi_escapes 0.2.1 (vte-based, the crate the baseline used at d230009 src/main.rs:794, Cargo.toml strip-ansi-escapes="0.2") strips BOTH of those inputs to "" — it restarts on the mid-sequence ESC. So the baseline got these right; the c1/m16 rewrite regressed them. This is a genuine fidelity regression, not a pre-existing condition.

Blast radius is correctly bounded by the claim and I verified each part:
- raw.log is unaffected: logs.rs:58 writes the raw chunk verbatim (independent of AnsiCleaner).
- Only the clean log diverges: logs.rs:61-63 feeds chunk through AnsiCleaner into clean_log, which is what read/--tail reads.
- The vt100 screen snapshot is unaffected (vt100 is vte-based and restarts on ESC), consistent with baseline.
- No real text loss / no UTF-8 corruption: the leaked bytes are printable ASCII ("0m"/"[0m").

Severity = low (downgraded from the claimed medium): the consequence is a few stray printable characters in the human-readable clean.log / read output. It requires an aborted/interrupted control sequence immediately followed by a fresh ESC — an uncommon, edge-case input, not everyday output. There is no data loss, no security-boundary impact, and an easy faithful fallback exists (raw.log is the ground-truth record). It is still a legitimate finding because the baseline handled it correctly, but the magnitude (cosmetic, narrow-trigger, recoverable) caps it at low / "misleading-output UX with an easy workaround."

finding.title: stop during startup can be silently undone: supervise_pty re-promotes a just-stopped session to Running
finding.detail: Scenario (daemon mode, also direct): a session is Starting. The user runs `stop s`. stop_session_silent (session.rs:604) loads metadata=Starting, signals whatever PIDs are recorded (often none yet), then mark_stopped writes status=Stopped and the client prints 'stopped session s'. Concurrently the supervise_pty thread is between its metadata load at session.rs:356 and its save at session.rs:368: it read status=Starting (before the user's mark_stopped committed), passed the Stopped-check at session.rs:360, and now writes status=Running with the live child_pid at session.rs:364-368, overwriting the user's Stopped. Observable consequence: the user received a success 'stopped' message, but the session is now Running with a live child that keeps consuming input/producing output; the stop was lost. Versus baseline: baseline supervise_pty had NO Stopped-check and ALWAYS set Running after spawn, so this resurrection happened unconditionally; the m11/m24 fix narrowed it to a check-then-write race but did not make the Starting->Running transition atomic against a concurrent mark_stopped, so the failure still occurs under interleaving.
finding.anchor: supervise_pty, src/session.rs:356-368 (load/check-Stopped/set-Running is not atomic w.r.t. concurrent mark_stopped in stop_session_silent)
finding.fix: Serialize the Starting->Running promotion against stop using the existing per-session start lock (or a compare-and-set on status) so supervise_pty only sets Running if it still observes Starting under the lock, and stop only marks Stopped under the same lock.

verdict.reasoning:
The race is real and reachable. supervise_pty's Starting->Running promotion is a non-atomic load/check/write: it reads metadata at src/session.rs:356, checks status==Stopped at :360, and only later writes status=Running + the live child_pid at :364-368 via save_metadata -> write_atomic (last-writer-wins whole-file rename, confirmed in paths.rs:140-173; no CAS, no lock). stop_session_silent reads metadata at :606, early-returns only if it already sees Stopped (:610), otherwise signals recorded PIDs and writes status=Stopped via mark_stopped at :636. Nothing serializes these two writers.

The per-session start lock does NOT cover this. acquire_start_lock is taken only in the two start handlers (session.rs:93 and daemon.rs:223) and is held by the start *request* path through wait_for_running_metadata. supervise_pty runs in a separate spawned thread (daemon.rs:236) / separate supervisor process (direct mode) and never takes the lock; stop_session_silent never takes it either. flock is advisory and only blocks other flock callers, so the start handler holding start.lock provides zero mutual exclusion against stop.

Daemon-mode failure interleaving, step by step against current lines:
1. start s: start_session_in_daemon initializes status=Starting (daemon.rs:231) and spawns supervisor thread B.
2. Thread B (supervise_pty) opens the FIFO (:349) then loads metadata (:356) and observes Starting (the user's stop has not committed yet); passes the Stopped-check at :360.
3. stop s runs in handler thread C: stop_session_silent loads metadata (:606), sees Starting (not Stopped, so no early return at :610). In daemon mode the recorded supervisor_pid and child_pid are both None at this instant (daemon never sets supervisor_pid; B writes child_pid only at :365), so both pid_is_ours checks (:618,:625) are false and C signals nothing. C then commits status=Stopped via mark_stopped (:636) and the client prints "stopped session 's'".
4. Thread B resumes and writes status=Running with the live child_pid at :364-368, clobbering C's Stopped. B proceeds to spawn the I/O threads and child.wait() (:383). Neither capture_output nor forward_input (logs.rs) ever re-reads status, and wait_for_running_metadata in thread A now sees Running and returns success.

Observable consequence: the user received the success message "stopped session 's'" but the session is Running with a live child (e.g. claude / a REPL) that keeps consuming FIFO input and producing output until it exits on its own. The stop was silently lost.

Reachability/likelihood: the "stop arrives while Starting" precondition is easy (the whole startup window, up to ~5s of polling). The hard part is the stop's metadata rename landing in the narrow gap between B's load (:356) and B's save (:368) -- a window of only a handful of syscalls (a now_unix() and the temp-create/write/flush/rename of write_atomic), realistically tens to low-hundreds of microseconds. Outside that gap the outcome is safe: a stop committing before B's load is honored by the :360 Stopped-check and B bails ("was stopped during startup", child reaped by the outer guard at :407-410); a stop after B's save sees Running and terminates the child. Only the thin middle interleaving loses the stop. Direct mode is largely self-correcting because supervisor_pid is set before supervise_pty (run_supervisor :286), so a racing stop SIGTERMs the supervisor's process group and kills the child anyway; the clean failure is daemon mode.

Regression framing: the claim's `sources: [regression]` is technically inaccurate -- baseline (d230009) supervise_pty had NO Stopped-check at all (main.rs:738-743: load -> set Running -> save unconditionally) and baseline stop_session_silent had no already-Stopped early-return (main.rs:1078-1099), so the same TOCTOU existed and resurrected even more readily. The m11/m24 fix did not break a working path; it narrowed the window by honoring a stop that commits before B's load. So this is an incomplete fix (the claim's `kind: incomplete-fix` is the correct characterization), not a true regression. Either way the issue is genuine.

Severity low: wrong observable behavior (a false "stopped" while the child runs) but only under a sub-millisecond race; the child is still tracked via the recorded child_pid (not orphaned/leaked), and recovery is trivial -- a second `stop s` succeeds, `status s` reveals Running, and daemon `shutdown` (which includes Running sessions) also reaps it. No data loss and no permanently stale state.
verdict.fix: Serialize the Starting->Running promotion against stop with a dedicated per-session status lock that the start *handler* does NOT hold (reusing start.lock directly would deadlock: in daemon mode the start handler holds start.lock with LOCK_NB across wait_for_running_metadata, so supervise_pty re-acquiring it would fail EWOULDBLOCK). Add e.g. acquire_status_lock(session_dir) -> flock(LOCK_EX, blocking) on <session_dir>/status.lock, then: (a) in supervise_pty hold it across lines 356-368 (load, the Stopped-check, and the save_metadata that writes Running) so the promotion only commits if it still observes a non-Stopped status under the lock; and (b) in stop_session_silent hold the same lock across the load at :606 through mark_stopped at :636 (load status, signal PIDs, write Stopped). Because both writers mutate status under the same exclusive lock and the start handler never takes it, the promotion and the stop can no longer interleave: a stop that commits during startup is observed by supervise_pty's :360 check (so it bails and the outer guard reaps the child), and a stop that arrives after promotion observes Running and terminates the child. A lighter alternative is a compare-and-set: re-read status immediately before the Running write and skip the write if it is no longer Starting -- but that still has a (smaller) TOCTOU unless done under a lock, so the dedicated status lock is the robust fix.

finding.title: Output-capture thread (and its log/PTY fds) leaks for the daemon's lifetime when the 2s drain cap expires
finding.detail: supervise_pty spawns output_thread for capture_output (session.rs:376) and, after the child exits, waits for it only via a bounded is_finished poll with a 2s deadline (session.rs:397-400); it never joins the handle. Scenario: a session whose command backgrounds a process that inherits the PTY slave, e.g. --cmd "bash -lc 'sleep 600 & disown'"; bash exits immediately but the slave fd stays open via the surviving sleep, so capture_output never sees EOF and output_thread.is_finished() stays false. The deadline expires, supervise_pty RETURNS Ok, and the JoinHandle is dropped — detaching a thread that still holds the cloned PTY master reader fd plus the raw.log and clean.log fds and keeps appending to the logs and rewriting screen.txt (for up to vt100's lifetime) for a session now marked Stopped. In daemon mode this leaks one thread and several fds per such session for the daemon's whole lifetime, trending toward fd/thread exhaustion, and produces the surprising effect of a Stopped session whose logs and screen snapshot keep changing. Baseline blocked on the reader (no leak, though it could hang forever — the bug m19 fixed); the m4/m19 drain-cap fix traded the hang for this detached-thread/fd leak and continued writes.
finding.anchor: supervise_pty drain loop, src/session.rs:397-400 (output_thread spawned at src/session.rs:376 is never joined when it does not finish within 2s)
finding.fix: Signal capture_output to stop (a shared AtomicBool it polls, or force the reader closed by dropping the cloned reader / closing the master fd) so it hits EOF, then join it, instead of abandoning the thread; alternatively cap total run length and abort the reader so its fds are released.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. supervise_pty (src/session.rs:370-400) clones the PTY master reader (master.try_clone_reader()) and hands it to output_thread running capture_output, then after child.wait() it does drop(master) and waits for output_thread ONLY via a bounded is_finished() poll with a 2s deadline (session.rs:397-400) — it never joins the handle, so on timeout the JoinHandle is dropped and the thread is detached.

Verified fd semantics in portable-pty 0.8.1 (~/.cargo/registry/.../portable-pty-0.8.1/src/unix.rs): try_clone_reader (line 314) calls self.fd.try_clone(), i.e. a dup() producing an INDEPENDENT fd; UnixMasterPty holds its own single fd closed on its own Drop. So drop(master) at session.rs:391 closes the supervisor's master fd but NOT the reader thread's dup'd fd. PtyFd::read (unix.rs:86-98) returns EOF (Ok(0)) only on EIO, which a PTY master raises only when the LAST slave fd is closed.

Empirically validated the crux with a throwaway C probe under /tmp using openpty+fork: after the immediate child is reaped while a grandchild keeps the slave open, the master read drains the buffered bytes and then never reaches EOF (returns EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK for >2s, never read==0, never EIO). In capture_output (src/logs.rs:23-73) the read is blocking with no stop flag (its only loop exits are read==0 or a read error), so the thread blocks in reader.read() indefinitely while still holding the dup'd master read fd plus the raw.log and clean.log append fds (logs.rs:24-35) and the vt100 parser, and will resume appending to the logs / rewriting screen.txt (logs.rs:58-71) if the grandchild emits more output — for a session already marked Stopped (daemon.rs:243).

Scenario: a session whose command backgrounds a slave-inheriting process, e.g. --cmd "bash -lc 'sleep 600 & disown'". bash exits, child.wait() returns, the 2s deadline expires, supervise_pty returns Ok, output_thread is detached. In daemon mode (start_session_in_daemon, daemon.rs:236) supervise_pty runs in a daemon worker thread; that worker exits after mark_stopped but the detached output_thread outlives it and the daemon never exits, so one thread + ~3 fds leak per such session for the daemon's lifetime, trending toward fd/thread exhaustion. No recovery: the session is already Stopped so a later stop no-ops (session.rs:610), and capture_output has no signal path.

REGRESSION confirmed: baseline src/main.rs at d230009 (lines 760-763) did drop(pair.master) then let _ = output_thread.join() — it blocked until capture_output returned, so no detached-thread/fd leak (its flaw was the indefinite hang that m4/m19 fixed). The fix replaced the unconditional join with a bounded is_finished() poll and abandons the handle on timeout, trading the hang for this thread/fd leak plus continued writes to a Stopped session.

Refutation attempts that FAILED: (a) "drop(master) closes the reader's fd and forces EOF" — false; the reader is an independent dup, only the master handle's own fd is closed. (b) "the master read returns EOF when the child exits" — false; PTY master EOF requires all slave fds closed, and a surviving grandchild holds one (probe-verified). (c) "capture_output polls a stop flag like forward_input" — false; capture_output takes no stop flag and its read is blocking.

Severity low (matches claim): needs the specific pattern of a command that backgrounds a PTY-slave-inheriting process; the consequence is gradual fd/thread accumulation in a long-lived daemon plus a surprising Stopped-session-with-mutating-logs effect, not data loss or a broken security boundary, and there is an easy workaround (restart the daemon).
verdict.fix: Plumb a stop signal into capture_output AND force the reader to actually unblock (a flag alone won't wake a blocked blocking read). Concretely in supervise_pty: open the cloned reader with O_NONBLOCK (or, simpler and reliable, after child.wait() set O_NONBLOCK on the reader fd via fcntl), pass an Arc<AtomicBool> stop flag to capture_output, and have capture_output's loop treat WouldBlock as "no data" and re-check stop (sleeping briefly), returning Ok when stop is set. Then after the 2s drain window set stop = true and output_thread.join() unconditionally so the thread, its dup'd master read fd, and the raw.log/clean.log fds are released. Equivalently: keep the master reader fd reachable (do not move it wholly into the thread) and, on timeout, force EOF by closing/shutting the dup'd reader fd so the blocked read returns, then join. Either way the JoinHandle must be joined, never dropped, so no thread/fd is abandoned for the daemon's lifetime and a Stopped session's logs/screen stop changing.

finding.title: Daemon shutdown stops sessions serially inside the client connection and can exceed the client's 60s timeout
finding.detail: handle_daemon_request runs shutdown_sessions_for_daemon synchronously in the connection handler and only writes the response after every session is stopped (daemon.rs:188-191, 251-292). Each stop_session_silent calls terminate_pid, which waits up to 20x100ms for SIGTERM then up to 20x100ms for SIGKILL = ~4s per child that ignores SIGTERM (session.rs:648-665), processed one session at a time. The client sets a 60s read timeout (protocol.rs, CLIENT_IO_TIMEOUT). With on the order of ~15+ sessions whose children ignore SIGTERM (or fewer plus other latency), the daemon does not produce the response within 60s, so the client raises 'daemon accepted the connection but did not respond within 60s; it may be suspended or wedged — restart it or rerun with --direct' even though the daemon is healthy and the shutdown is in fact proceeding/completing. Misleading error with an easy workaround (the shutdown still happens), but it directly contradicts the diagnostic the m13/m15 timeouts were meant to provide.
finding.anchor: shutdown_sessions_for_daemon serial terminate_pid loop, src/daemon.rs:281-286 (per-child wait in terminate_pid, src/session.rs:648-665) vs client timeout, src/protocol.rs (CLIENT_IO_TIMEOUT)
finding.fix: Acknowledge the shutdown request quickly (reply before the slow termination, or stop sessions concurrently / with a shorter aggregate bound) so the client's 60s window is not blocked by serial SIGTERM/SIGKILL grace periods.

verdict.reasoning:
The mechanism the claim describes is real and present in current code, though one number in the claim is overstated.

Verified scenario path:
1. handle_daemon_stream runs the work first, then replies: `let response = handle_daemon_request(request); write_daemon_response(&mut stream, &response)?;` (src/daemon.rs:137-138). There is no early acknowledgment of a Shutdown — the response is written only after handle_daemon_request returns.
2. For Shutdown, handle_daemon_request calls shutdown_sessions_for_daemon (src/daemon.rs:188), which collects all Running/Starting sessions and stops them in a strictly serial loop: `for name in sessions { stop_session_silent(&name) ... }` (src/daemon.rs:281-286). No concurrency, no aggregate time bound.
3. Each stop_session_silent → terminate_pid (src/session.rs:604-639, 641-668). For a child that ignores SIGTERM, the first loop runs all 20 iterations × 100ms = ~2.0s before SIGKILL is sent (src/session.rs:648-653). In daemon mode the supervisor_pid is None (initialize_session_files sets it None at src/session.rs:216; supervise_pty, called directly by the daemon thread at src/daemon.rs:236-238, only sets child_pid — only run_supervisor in direct mode sets supervisor_pid at src/session.rs:285), so pid_is_ours(supervisor_pid=None,...) is false and terminate_pid is invoked once per session (the child only). Good — the daemon does not signal its own PID.
4. Client sets a 60s read timeout (CLIENT_IO_TIMEOUT, src/protocol.rs:18, 80-85) and, on WouldBlock/TimedOut, raises exactly the message the claim quotes: "...accepted the connection but did not respond within 60s; it may be suspended or wedged — restart it or rerun with --direct" (src/protocol.rs:95-109).
5. Baseline (d230009) had NO client read timeout (git show d230009:src/main.rs try_send_daemon_request just does read_line with no set_read_timeout), so the misleading diagnostic is new with the m13/m15 fix; baseline simply blocked until completion.

Correction to the claim's arithmetic: per-child worst case is ~2.0s, not ~4s. I probed kill(pid,0) on a zombie (/tmp/zombie_probe): it returns 0 (process_alive=true) until the parent reaps, then ESRCH. In the shutdown path the daemon's own supervise_pty thread is blocked on child.wait() (src/session.rs:383) and reaps the child immediately after SIGKILL, so the second 20×100ms loop converges in ~1 iteration rather than running full. Thus ~2s per SIGTERM-ignoring child, so roughly ~30 such concurrent sessions are needed to exceed 60s (not ~15). The claim hedges this ("~15+ ... or fewer plus other latency"), and SIGTERM-ignoring children are a real category here (interactive shells/REPLs ignore SIGTERM; some dev servers trap it), so the qualitative failure stands: a healthy daemon completing a large serial shutdown can exceed the client's 60s window and trip the "suspended/wedged — restart it" diagnostic, directly contradicting what that diagnostic is supposed to mean.

Severity low is correct: misleading-error UX with an easy workaround — the shutdown actually completes; re-running `agent-bridge shutdown` then reports "daemon not running". It is not a regression of previously-correct behavior (baseline blocked forever with no diagnostic, which is no better), so no severity bump applies. The DAEMON-side 30s write timeout does not bound the serial loop; nothing caps the aggregate shutdown duration.
verdict.fix: Acknowledge the shutdown before doing the slow termination, OR bound/parallelize the stops so the client's 60s window is never blocked by serial SIGTERM/SIGKILL grace periods. Minimal option: in handle_daemon_stream, for DaemonRequest::Shutdown, write the success response (e.g. "daemon shutting down\n") FIRST, then run shutdown_sessions_for_daemon, then exit — the client already only needs the ack to know shutdown was accepted. Alternatively, in shutdown_sessions_for_daemon spawn one thread per session so the wall-clock cost is ~max(per-child) instead of sum, and/or shorten terminate_pid's per-phase grace (e.g. SIGTERM grace 1s, SIGKILL grace 1s) for the shutdown path. The reply-first approach is smallest and removes the false "wedged" diagnostic entirely while the daemon still reaps/terminates children before std::process::exit(0).

finding.title: child_path drops the client's forwarded PATH entirely if BaseDirs::new() fails, reverting a daemon session to the daemon's PATH
finding.detail: child_path(Some(client_path)) calls BaseDirs::new()? (paths.rs:249) purely to locate ~/.local/bin for prepending; if BaseDirs::new() returns None (HOME unset and no usable passwd entry), the `?` makes child_path return None even though a perfectly good client PATH was supplied. supervise_pty then skips command.env("PATH", ...) (session.rs:332-334) and the child does not get the client's PATH at all — it inherits the daemon's process environment PATH via portable-pty's get_base_env snapshot instead. The m18 fix's whole point was to run daemon sessions with the user's PATH; under this edge it silently reverts to the daemon's PATH, so a command that resolves in the user's shell can fail to resolve (or resolve to a different binary) in the session. Rare (requires a degenerate environment) but a silent functional regression of the m18 guarantee.
finding.fix: Do not gate the whole client PATH on BaseDirs success: if the home dir is unavailable, return the client PATH unmodified (skip only the ~/.local/bin prepend) rather than None.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's mechanics are accurate and I traced the full scenario path against current code.

child_path (src/paths.rs:246-254): for the Some(client_path) branch it calls `let home = BaseDirs::new()?;` at line 249 purely to locate ~/.local/bin. If BaseDirs::new() returns None, the `?` makes child_path return None even though a usable client PATH was supplied. supervise_pty (src/session.rs:306-334) then has path == None, so (a) the resolve_program_path block at 307-311 is skipped (the bare command name is left in command_parts[0]) and (b) `command.env("PATH", path)` at 332-334 is skipped. The child therefore inherits the DAEMON's PATH: portable-pty CommandBuilder seeds envs from get_base_env() (portable-pty-0.8.1/src/cmdbuilder.rs:72-73 -> std::env::vars_os()), and its search_path/resolve_path (cmdbuilder.rs:406-410) resolves the bare name against that daemon PATH. So the m18 guarantee (daemon runs the session with the user's PATH) is silently not delivered, and a command that would resolve via the client PATH can fail to resolve or resolve to a different binary. Confirmed consequence is real.

When does BaseDirs::new() actually return None? directories 6.0.0 -> dirs_sys 0.5.0 home_dir() (lib.rs:33-52) uses $HOME first and only falls back to getpwuid_r; it returns None only when HOME is unset/empty AND getpwuid_r yields no usable home dir. So the trigger is a degenerate environment (synthetic/nobody UID or container with no passwd home AND HOME unset). It is additionally gated: child_path(Some) is only reachable in the daemon (the only Some caller is daemon.rs:238 start_session_in_daemon; the run_supervisor caller at session.rs:293 passes None). For the daemon to be running at all under unusable HOME/passwd it must have AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME set, because run_daemon (src/daemon.rs:38) calls bridge_root()? first and bridge_root_from falls back to BaseDirs::new() (paths.rs:54-ff) when AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME is unset. If AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME is unset and BaseDirs failed, the daemon never starts, so that sub-case can't reach the bug; the live trigger is therefore: AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME set + HOME unset + getpwuid_r has no home. Reachable but rare, and the observable failure only bites if the daemon's own PATH also lacks the binary.

Not a regression: at baseline (d230009) supervise_pty (main.rs:700-729) used path_with_local_bin() (main.rs:1231-1234), which also does BaseDirs::new()? and skips command.env("PATH", ...) on None — identical behavior in this exact edge, and baseline had no client-PATH concept to forward. So current is no worse than baseline; this is an incomplete fix / hardening gap, not a broken-previously-working path. That keeps it at low (concrete but extreme-edge failure with easy workarounds: set HOME, or put the tool on the daemon's PATH).

I confirm rather than refute because there IS a constructible scenario with an observable consequence (usable client PATH silently dropped, defeating m18's stated purpose for that case), and the trivial fix would prevent it; but severity stays low given the triple-degenerate trigger, the easy workaround, and that it does not regress baseline.

finding.title: doctor's login-shell comparison hardcodes zsh, ignoring the user's actual login shell so the mismatch warning is wrong or never fires off zsh
finding.detail: The behavior-based doctor (c4) probes the login shell with Command::new("zsh").args(["-lic", "command -v claude; claude --version"]) at doctor.rs:69-72 to compare where the login shell resolves claude against Agent Bridge's resolution, then derives a load-bearing warning at doctor.rs:88-94 ('login shell resolves claude to X, but Agent Bridge resolves Y'). It hardcodes zsh rather than the user's $SHELL. For a user whose login shell is bash/fish, this reads zsh's dotfiles (not their .bash_profile/.zprofile PATH customizations), so the warning compares against the wrong shell and can be misleading or spuriously fire/not-fire. On a host without zsh on PATH (common on Linux, the stated future target, and on macOS accounts using bash/fish), Command::new("zsh") fails with ENOENT, print_command_output prints 'failed: ...', login_path becomes None, and the mismatch warning is silently skipped — the diagnostic intended to catch PATH mismatches becomes inert while showing a confusing error line. This is diagnostic-only output (the doctor subcommand), never a runtime session path, and has an easy workaround, but it is a real correctness/portability gap in code a fix introduced.
finding.anchor: doctor login-shell probe, src/doctor.rs:69-72 (warning derived at src/doctor.rs:88-94)
finding.fix: Resolve the login shell from std::env::var("SHELL") (falling back to a passwd/getpwuid lookup, then a sensible default) and invoke that binary with its own login-interactive flags, rather than hardcoding zsh; or skip the login-shell section with an explicit note when the detected login shell is unavailable.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed against current code in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/doctor.rs. The c4 fix (commit bc06198) hardcodes Command::new("zsh").args(["-lic", ...]) at lines 69-72 and makes its output load-bearing by deriving the mismatch warning at lines 88-94 from login_path = first non-empty stdout line. This is genuinely NEW logic: at baseline (d230009, main.rs:494-497) zsh was already invoked but its output was only PRINTED, never compared — the fix is what turned the parse into a warning, so it is in-scope re-review surface.

All three failure scenarios verified step-by-step:
(1) No zsh on PATH (Linux future target; macOS bash/fish accounts): Command::new("zsh") returns Err(ENOENT); line 73 `login.as_ref().ok()` -> None -> login_path None -> the `if let Some(login_path)` mismatch check at line 88 is silently skipped, while print_command_output prints `  failed: No such file or directory (os error 2)` (line 117). The PATH-mismatch diagnostic goes inert while showing a confusing error line. (As reported.)
(2) Non-zsh login shell with zsh installed: `-lic` sources ~/.zshenv/.zprofile/.zshrc, never the user's ~/.bash_profile/.bashrc/fish config (confirmed -i sources .zshrc via /tmp probe), so the comparison uses the wrong shell's PATH and the warning is misleading. (As reported.)
(3) NEW, stronger than reported — zsh IS the actual login shell and installed (the primary supported macOS config): I proved with a /tmp probe on this host that any ~/.zprofile or ~/.zshrc that writes to stdout (Powerlevel10k notices, nvm/pyenv/conda init banners, a greeting echo — all very common, and -i maximizes exposure) makes `.lines().next()` (doctor.rs:75) capture the NOISE line, not the `command -v claude` path. Line 78's `.filter(|line| !line.is_empty())` rejects only empty lines, not non-path lines, and nothing scopes the read to the command-v output. Result: login_path (e.g. "login profile loaded") != resolved_program (/opt/homebrew/bin/claude on this host) -> the warning SPURIOUSLY fires with nonsense: "login shell resolves claude to 'login profile loaded', but Agent Bridge resolves '/opt/homebrew/bin/claude'". So the warning is fragile even in the main supported configuration, not just off-zsh.

Coverage: tests/cli.rs:251-273 exercise doctor only with --cmd sh and a missing binary; neither hits command_is_claude, so the entire login-shell comparison branch (doctor.rs:56-95) is untested.

This is a REGRESSION-adjacent gap introduced by the fix (new parse logic), but it is diagnostic-only output of the `doctor` subcommand — never a runtime session-spawn path — with an easy workaround (the `claude --version` probe printed just above is the real health signal). No security boundary, data loss, or core-command-unusable consequence. Hence low per the rubric (misleading-error/UX with easy workaround), though scenario 3's spurious fire on the primary platform sits at the low/medium boundary.
verdict.fix:
Two-part fix; the report's $SHELL suggestion alone is insufficient because it does not address scenario 3 (first-line stdout noise poisons ANY login shell).

(a) Use the user's actual login shell instead of hardcoded zsh: read std::env::var("SHELL") (fall back to a getpwuid lookup, then "/bin/sh"), and invoke that binary with appropriate login-interactive flags. If the resolved shell binary is unavailable, skip the login-shell section with an explicit informational note (e.g. "login shell '<sh>' unavailable; skipping comparison") rather than emitting a `failed:` line and silently dropping the warning.

(b) Make the parse robust to dotfile stdout noise: do not treat `.lines().next()` as the path. Print a unique sentinel and read the line immediately after it, e.g. run the probe as `printf '__AB_CLAUDE__\n'; command -v claude` and set login_path to the first non-empty line that follows the `__AB_CLAUDE__` marker (a token user dotfiles cannot reproduce). Additionally guard the comparison so it only warns when login_path looks like an absolute path (starts with '/') and is_executable, avoiding spurious warnings from aliases/functions or leftover banner text.

Also add a test that exercises the claude branch (e.g. a fake `claude` on PATH plus a noisy fake login shell) asserting no spurious mismatch warning is produced.

finding.title: Direct-mode stop during startup kills the supervisor's group but the setsid'd PTY child survives orphaned and becomes unkillable
finding.detail: Direct mode: `agent-bridge --direct start s --cmd '...'` spawns the setsid supervisor; run_supervisor (session.rs:281) records supervisor_pid + start_time with status=Starting, then supervise_pty openpty's and spawn_command's the child (session.rs:336-339). The child does its OWN setsid() (portable-pty), so it lives in its own process group, not the supervisor's. There is a real window (FIFO open + load_metadata + Stopped-check at session.rs:360-362 + save Running at 368) during which the child PROCESS exists but child_pid is still None in metadata. A concurrent `agent-bridge --direct stop s` in that window: stop_session_silent (session.rs:604) loads status=Starting (not Stopped, so no early-return), sees child_pid=None so pid_is_ours(None,..)=false and skips the child, but supervisor_pid IS recorded so it calls terminate_pid(supervisor_pid). signal_pid_and_group signals -supervisor_pid (session.rs:674), which only reaches the supervisor's own group; the child setsid'd away and is NOT signaled. The single-threaded supervisor (default SIGTERM disposition) dies before it can run the reap guard at session.rs:407-410. Result: the PTY child is orphaned (reparented to launchd), keeps running and holding the PTY slave, while stop_session_silent then mark_stopped writes status=Stopped with child_pid never recorded. Observable: status/list report Stopped, and a subsequent stop early-returns at session.rs:610 because status==Stopped, so the orphan is UNKILLABLE via agent-bridge until it exits on its own. The m12 reap-if-marked-Stopped fix only covers the sub-case where stop marks Stopped before the supervisor reaches line 360; it does not cover stop terminating the supervisor mid-startup in direct mode.
finding.anchor: stop_session_silent terminating the supervisor (src/session.rs:625-630) interacting with the unrecorded-child window in supervise_pty (src/session.rs:336-368) and the reap guard the dead supervisor never runs (src/session.rs:407-410)
finding.fix: Record child_pid (and child_start_time) into metadata immediately after spawn_command and before opening the FIFO / promoting to Running, so a racing stop can identify and signal the child's own group directly; or have stop_session_silent, when status==Starting with a live supervisor but no child_pid, re-read child_pid after terminating the supervisor and signal it.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's signal-ROUTING mechanism is real, but its load-bearing CONSEQUENCE ("the PTY child keeps running, orphaned, unkillable") is prevented by a kernel mechanism the claim never accounts for, and the regression framing is false.

WHAT THE CLAIM GETS RIGHT (verified against current code):
- portable-pty 0.8.1 (~/.cargo/.../portable-pty-0.8.1/src/unix.rs:200-247) runs in the child's pre_exec: it resets SIGCHLD/SIGHUP/SIGINT/SIGQUIT/SIGTERM/SIGALRM to SIG_DFL (lines 208-217), then calls setsid() (line 220), then TIOCSCTTY (controlling_tty defaults true, cmdbuilder.rs:216; session.rs uses CommandBuilder::new). So the child becomes its OWN session/process-group leader, separate from the supervisor's group.
- The race window is real: in supervise_pty (session.rs:336-368) the child PROCESS exists after spawn_command (339) but metadata.child_pid stays None until save at 368. stop_session_silent (604) reads status=Starting (no early-return at 610), sees child_pid=None so pid_is_ours(None,..)=false and skips the child (618-623), and signals only the supervisor (625-630). signal_pid_and_group (674) sends to [-supervisor_pid, supervisor_pid] — neither reaches the setsid'd child. terminate_pid blocks until the supervisor dies (648-667) BEFORE mark_stopped (636), and the single-threaded supervisor (no SIGTERM handler; output/input threads not yet spawned) dies on default SIGTERM before reaching the reap guard at 407-410. So child_pid is never recorded; final metadata is status=Stopped, child_pid=None.

WHY THE CONSEQUENCE IS REFUTED (the mechanism the claim omits):
When the supervisor dies it closes its sole PTY MASTER fd (pair.master / `master` at session.rs:342; the reader/writer dups at 370-373 are not yet created in this window, so exactly one master fd is open). On both BSD/macOS and Linux, closing the last master fd of a pty delivers SIGHUP to the slave's controlling/foreground process group. The child set itself as that controlling process (setsid+TIOCSCTTY) and its SIGHUP disposition was reset to SIG_DFL by portable-pty. I empirically verified on this macOS host (/tmp probes): closing the master while a setsid+TIOCSCTTY child reads delivers SIGHUP, and a default-disposition child is KILLED by signal 1 (a SIG_IGN child survived with exit 1007). So for the overwhelmingly common case — cat, python3 -i, most REPLs/CLIs, and a Node app like claude that registers no SIGHUP listener — the supervisor's death reaps the child via SIGHUP. The orphan does NOT "keep running"; the claim's blanket assertion is false for normal commands.

NOT A REGRESSION (regression/incomplete-fix tag is unsupported): baseline d230009 stop_session_silent (main.rs:1078-1099) used the identical signal_pid_and_group([-pid, pid]) (main.rs:1133-1138) against the same setsid'd-child topology, recorded child_pid only after spawn (main.rs:741), and so in this exact window equally read child_pid=None and signaled only -supervisor_pid — the child was never reached at baseline either. The new early-return on Stopped (608-612) doesn't worsen it: child_pid is None in this window in BOTH versions, so a subsequent stop had nothing to signal at baseline too. The current code ADDED pid_is_ours gating (618/625) and the m12 reap guard (407-410) — strictly more protection, none removed. So the fixes neither introduced nor worsened this behavior.

RESIDUAL (narrow, pre-existing, not the claimed failure): a target program that explicitly ignores/handles SIGHUP would survive the master-close SIGHUP and then be unreachable via agent-bridge (no recorded child_pid). That is a genuine but much narrower edge that exists identically at baseline and is a property of the PTY design, not a fix regression. It does not support the claim as written (unconditional orphan survival / regression), so the verdict is refuted; the single fact that would flip the narrow subcase is whether the specific launched program installs a SIGHUP handler.
verdict.fix: No fix required for the claim as stated: the supervisor's death closes its only PTY master fd, and the kernel delivers SIGHUP to the setsid'd child (whose disposition portable-pty reset to SIG_DFL), so a default-disposition child is killed (verified on macOS: signal 1). This is also identical to baseline behavior, so it is not a regression. OPTIONAL hardening for the narrow subclass of programs that explicitly ignore/handle SIGHUP (which would otherwise survive as an orphan with no recorded child_pid): record child_pid + child_start_time into metadata immediately after spawn_command (session.rs:339) and before opening the FIFO/promoting to Running, so a racing direct-mode stop can identify and signal the child's own process group directly; or have stop_session_silent, when status==Starting with a live supervisor but no recorded child_pid, re-read metadata after terminating the supervisor and signal any child_pid the supervisor managed to record. Neither is necessary to prevent the claimed everyday failure.

finding.title: Full-disk capture write failures silently blind the manager agent: all session output is discarded with no client-visible signal
finding.detail: The m20 resilience change makes capture_output treat every sink write error as warn-once-and-continue (logs.rs:54-71): on a full disk (ENOSPC) the raw log, clean log, AND screen snapshot all stop being written after a single eprintln to the daemon's stderr, while the loop keeps draining the PTY. The session stays Running and write_session_bytes keeps succeeding, so a manager agent can still send/keys, but every read/screen returns stale or empty content with ok=true and no error. The only failure signal (warn_once) goes to the daemon process's stderr, which a programmatic driver never sees. Net effect under a plausible condition (disk fills while a session runs): the agent drives the session completely blind — it cannot tell its view is frozen — and all transcript output for that period is lost. This is the intended 'don't wedge the child' tradeoff, but the lack of any client-observable indication turns a recoverable disk-full condition into silent, undiagnosable data loss for the consumer.
finding.anchor: capture_output warn_once handling, src/logs.rs:54-71 (warn_once at src/logs.rs:75)
finding.fix: Surface persistent capture write failures into session state (e.g. record a 'capture degraded: <reason>' marker in metadata or an exit_status-style field) so status/read can report to the client that output is being dropped, instead of only logging once to daemon stderr.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's factual mechanics are accurate, but the conclusion (that this is a fix-introduced finding) is wrong: it is the explicitly-excluded intentional tradeoff and is not a regression.

VERIFIED FACTS (all true):
- capture_output (src/logs.rs:54-71) warn-once-and-continues per sink on write error; on ENOSPC the raw log, clean log, and screen snapshot (via write_atomic, src/logs.rs:67) all stop updating while the loop keeps draining the PTY.
- The session stays Running: supervise_pty only reaches mark_stopped when child.wait() returns (src/session.rs:383; daemon.rs:243). A draining-but-failing capture never stops the child.
- write_session_bytes (src/session.rs:452-482) checks only status==Running and writes the FIFO; it never touches the capture sinks, so send/keys keep returning ok=true.
- read_output_text/read_screen_text/status_text read the now-stale files; the daemon wraps them as ok=true,error=None (daemon.rs:181-199). No error reaches the client.
- The only signal, warn_once's eprintln (src/logs.rs:75-82), goes to supervisor/daemon stderr. In direct mode the supervisor is spawned .stderr(Stdio::null()) (session.rs:118); in daemon mode capture runs inside the daemon whose stderr a programmatic client never reads. So a socket consumer sees nothing.

WHY THIS IS NOT A FINDING:
1) NOT A REGRESSION. At baseline d230009, capture_output `?`-propagated a write error, but that error returned into output_thread's JoinHandle and was DISCARDED by `let _ = output_thread.join();` (baseline main.rs:763). The supervisor was blocked on child.wait() (baseline main.rs:760) and only marked the session Stopped when the child independently exited, recording the CHILD's exit_status — never the capture error. So reads/screen returned stale content with ok=true and no error AT BASELINE TOO — identical client-observability. Worse, because the reader thread had exited, the PTY buffer would fill and the child would wedge (frozen zombie still reporting Running). The m20 fix did not remove any pre-existing client signal (there was none) and STRICTLY IMPROVED the failure mode by continuing to drain. Even the intermediate pre-m20 modular code discards output_thread's Result (it only polls is_finished(), session.rs:398-400), so no version ever surfaced a capture error to the client. The rule "REGRESSION = the fix broke something that worked at baseline" does not apply.

2) INTENTIONAL TRADEOFF + ROADMAP ENHANCEMENT. The prompt names the m20 continue-on-write-error as the intended "don't wedge the child" tradeoff and excludes such intentional/correct design choices and design-doc future gaps from being findings. The claim itself concedes "This is the intended 'don't wedge the child' tradeoff." Its proposed remedy is to ADD a new capability (a "capture degraded" metadata/exit_status field) — an enhancement, not a bug fix. The fix does exactly what it was designed to do, correctly.

3) THE PROPOSED REMEDY IS DEFEATED BY THE CONDITION. On a full disk, persisting a "capture degraded" marker via write_atomic to metadata (session.rs:712-718) would itself fail with ENOSPC, so the suggested signal could not be reliably recorded. This confirms the stale-reads behavior is an inherent property of the durable-file design under disk exhaustion, not a localized defect in capture_output.

The fix is complete and correct; no concrete bug is introduced or left by it.
verdict.fix: No fix required. The behavior is the intended m20 "don't wedge the child" tradeoff and is not a regression: baseline d230009 (and every intermediate version) had identical client-observability (stale reads, ok=true, no error) because the output thread's Result was always discarded, and the session only stops on child exit. m20 strictly improved the failure mode by continuing to drain the PTY instead of letting the child wedge. Surfacing disk-full degradation to clients is a roadmap-style enhancement (and is partly self-defeating, since persisting a marker also fails under ENOSPC), not a defect in the fix.

finding.title: On a Unix that is neither macOS nor Linux, the new PID-reuse defense silently degrades to bare liveness for every session
finding.detail: procinfo::process_start_time has three cfg blocks: macOS (proc_pidinfo), Linux (/proc/<pid>/stat), and not(macos/linux) which unconditionally returns None (procinfo.rs:53-58). The m5/c2 fix made pid_is_ours (session.rs:739-742) treat a recorded start-time token of None as 'fall back to a bare liveness probe.' On a current build for any third Unix (e.g. FreeBSD/illumos), run_supervisor (session.rs:287) and supervise_pty (session.rs:366) therefore ALWAYS record None for supervisor_start_time/child_start_time, so EVERY session — not just legacy metadata — loses the PID-reuse protection the fix added: after an unclean shutdown a recycled PID is accepted as 'ours', and stop_session_silent (session.rs:618-630) would then SIGTERM/SIGKILL that PID and its whole process group. The team's stated scope is 'macOS now, Linux later', so a BSD build is not a declared target and CI would not exercise it; on the two real targets the tokens are populated and the defense holds. This is distinct from the prompt's explicitly-excluded legacy-metadata case (an old file): here it is a freshly written file on an unsupported OS.
finding.anchor: platform::process_start_time not(macos/linux) arm, src/procinfo.rs:53-58 (consumed by pid_is_ours, src/session.rs:739-742)
finding.fix: Either add a compile_error!/build cfg guard restricting the crate to macos+linux until another backend exists, or have callers refuse to promote a session to Running when process_start_time returns None on an otherwise-Unix platform so PID-reuse safety is never silently dropped.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's CODE mechanism is real and verified, but under this review's explicit rules it is NOT an in-scope finding. Refuting mechanism by mechanism:

VERIFIED FACTS (all true):
- procinfo.rs:53-58: the not(any(macos,linux)) arm returns None unconditionally with no compile_error!. Every other platform call in src/ is POSIX-portable libc (setsid session.rs:124; flock session.rs:178/daemon.rs:84; O_NONBLOCK/fcntl session.rs:352/465/490/495; ENXIO session.rs:468; geteuid paths.rs:93; O_NOFOLLOW paths.rs:186) — all present on FreeBSD/illumos via the libc crate. So a third-Unix build genuinely compiles and process_start_time always returns None there.
- run_supervisor records supervisor_start_time = process_start_time(pid) (session.rs:287); supervise_pty records child_start_time = child.process_id().and_then(process_start_time) (session.rs:366) — both None on a third Unix.
- pid_is_ours treats None as the bare-liveness arm: None => true (session.rs:739-742).
- stop_session_silent (session.rs:618-630) -> terminate_pid (646) -> signal_pid_and_group (670-687) signals Pid::from_raw(-pid) AND from_raw(pid), so a recycled PID and its group would be hit.

WHY THIS IS REFUTED (three independent, each sufficient, grounds — the claim concedes the first two):

1. EXPLICITLY EXCLUDED BY THE PROMPT. The 'WHAT IS NOT A FINDING' list names 'pid_is_ours falling back to bare liveness when no start-time token was recorded.' The third-Unix case IS exactly that fallback, reached through the deliberately-written None-returning arm. The '(legacy metadata only)' parenthetical describes the expected trigger on the SUPPORTED platforms; it does not turn the same intentional None-fallback into a bug on a platform the design never targeted. The behavior the design intentionally accepts (no token => bare liveness) is identical whether the None comes from old metadata or from the third-Unix cfg arm.

2. NO FAILURE ON A SUPPORTED PATH. Project scope is fixed as 'macOS now, Linux later,' and the rubric requires 'a concrete failure scenario with observable consequence.' On both declared targets the tokens ARE populated (macOS arm procinfo.rs:19-39 and Linux arm procinfo.rs:44-50 both return Some), so the defense holds. The scenario manifests only on FreeBSD/illumos — never built, shipped, or CI'd.

3. NOT A REGRESSION. Verified against baseline d230009:src/main.rs: zero start-time machinery (grep: only the group-kill at line 1137 matched), bare liveness everywhere (is_some_and(|pid| process_alive) at 538/596/964/1018), and stop_session_silent (line 1078) signaled any recorded Some(child_pid)/Some(supervisor_pid) UNCONDITIONALLY (lines 1083/1089) with the same from_raw(-pid) group-kill. So baseline accepted (and signaled) recycled PIDs on EVERY platform, with no identity OR liveness gate in stop. The current code on a third Unix is strictly NO WORSE — it additionally requires the PID to be alive (pid_is_ours' process_alive gate, session.rs:736) before signaling. The fix improved macOS+Linux and left a third Unix no-worse-than-baseline; nothing that worked was broken, so the 'regression is at least the severity of what it broke' clause does not apply.

The claim itself frames its value purely as hardening on an undeclared target — below the prompt's stated bar ('concrete failure scenario with observable consequence' on a supported path). The first verifier's 'uncertain' rested solely on declining to apply the rubric's scope/exclusion clauses; those clauses are unambiguous here. Mechanism real, but not an in-scope finding: intentionally-accepted None-fallback, undeclared platform, no regression. Verdict: refuted.
verdict.fix: No fix required for in-scope correctness. If the maintainers want to harden against a future BSD/illumos build silently shipping with degraded PID-reuse protection (defense-in-depth only, not a fix for any supported-platform defect), the cheapest guard is a compile-time fence in procinfo.rs alongside the cfg arms, e.g. add `#[cfg(not(any(target_os = "macos", target_os = "linux")))] compile_error!("agent-bridge has no process_start_time backend for this OS; PID-reuse defense would degrade to bare liveness");` so an unsupported target fails the build loudly instead of compiling into the None-fallback. This makes the 'macОS now, Linux later' scope explicit in the code rather than implicit.
